ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a burrito class (FOODON_00002764); no dedicated chimichanga subclass exists. Beef chimichanga is a deep-fried variant of a beef burrito, so the burrito class is used as the closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Burrito) is the closest available category. No dedicated chimichanga code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy; chimichanga is a deep-fried burrito variant.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for chimichanga or deep-fried burrito was identified in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of beef chimichangas is one piece weighing approximately 200 g, as commonly served in Mexican-American restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Chimichanga, beef and cheese — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 21321)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for beef-and-cheese chimichanga (NDB 21321) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values represent a standard deep-fried beef chimichanga with cheese filling. Minor rounding applied for per-100 g presentation. This is the closest approved-source match for a restaurant-style beef chimichanga as served in Singapore.

gi_source.title: Burrito with beef and beans — Glycemic Index estimation based on analogous mixed wheat-flour and legume dish; reference: Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC. International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002. Am J Clin Nutr. 2002;76(1):5–56.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for beef chimichanga was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated from the closest analogous food: a wheat-flour tortilla-based mixed dish with beef and beans. Wheat flour tortilla GI is approximately 30–52 (low); the mixed filling with beans and beef moderates the overall GI. A conservative estimate of 52 (upper boundary of low GI) is applied. GL per serving = (52 × 24.6 × 200/100) / 100 = 25.6 g. Documented as analogous estimate per Project Veritas v2 GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (490 mg per 100 g; approximately 980 mg per standard 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit beef chimichanga consumption due to its high sodium content (~980 mg per serving, representing approximately 43% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit), and should request reduced-sodium preparations or limit portion size to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~25.6 per serving) driven by refined wheat flour tortilla and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard beef chimichanga serving delivers a moderate glycemic load of approximately 25.6 g; portion control to half a serving and pairing with non-starchy vegetables can help attenuate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (4.6 g per 100 g), trans fat (0.3 g per 100 g), cholesterol (28 mg per 100 g), and high sodium from deep-frying and cheese filling
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit beef chimichanga intake due to its combined saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium burden; a single 200 g serving provides approximately 9.2 g saturated fat and 0.6 g trans fat, both of which contribute to atherogenic risk and should be minimised per AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef chimichangas are a deep-fried Mexican-American dish consisting of a large wheat flour tortilla filled with seasoned ground beef, refried beans, shredded cheddar cheese, onion, garlic, and spices such as cumin and chili powder. The filled tortilla is folded into a rectangular parcel and submerged in hot oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crispy, while the interior remains moist and savoury. The dish originated in the Sonoran region of northern Mexico and the southwestern United States, particularly Arizona, and is a staple of Tex-Mex and Mexican-American restaurant menus. In Singapore, beef chimichangas are served at specialist Mexican restaurants such as Three Amigos, typically as a main course priced around SGD 27. Nutritionally, a 200 g serving provides approximately 504 kcal, 21 g protein, 25.6 g fat, and 49.2 g carbohydrate. The dish is energy-dense due to deep-frying and the cheese filling. It carries a high sodium load (~980 mg per serving) and moderate glycemic load (~25.6), making it a food of concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The crispy exterior and rich, spiced beef-and-cheese interior give chimichangas a distinctive contrast of textures. They are commonly served with sour cream, guacamole, salsa, and shredded lettuce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a beef chimichanga?
What is the difference between a burrito and a chimichanga?
Where to eat chimichangas in Singapore?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef-and-cheese chimichanga (NDB 21321), which is the closest approved-source match. The GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable wheat-flour tortilla dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Foster-Powell et al. 2002 international GI table. No exact chimichanga entry was found in a primary regional source (HPB, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia). Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct chimichanga GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_size_g / 100) / 100 = (52 × 24.6 × 200 / 100) / 100 = 25.6. The input record listed cuisine_tags including 'Naan', which is inconsistent with a Mexican dish; this tag has been disregarded as likely a platform data artefact from the multi-cuisine restaurant context. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification; the dish contains beef which may or may not be halal-certified at the serving restaurant. The food is not a Singaporean dish; therefore the Singaporean multilingual naming requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. Mexican cuisine does not carry mandatory non-English local-language requirements under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules. The romanized Spanish name 'chimichanga de res' is included as a romanized alias.
